title: Bitwise Balancing
description: You are given three non-negative integers $$$b$$$, $$$c$$$, and $$$d$$$. Please find a non-negative integer $$$a \in [0, 2^{61}]$$$ such that $$$(a\, |\, b)-(a\, \&\, c)=d$$$, where $$$|$$$ and $$$\&$$$ denote the bitwise OR operation and the bitwise AND operation , respectively. If such an $$$a$$$ exists, print its value. If there is no solution, print a single integer $$$-1$$$. If there are multiple solutions, print any of them.
input_spec: Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^5$$$). The description of the test cases follows. The only line of each test case contains three positive integers $$$b$$$, $$$c$$$, and $$$d$$$ ($$$0 \le b, c, d \le 10^{18}$$$).
output_spec: For each test case, output the value of $$$a$$$, or $$$-1$$$ if there is no solution. Please note that $$$a$$$ must be non-negative and cannot exceed $$$2^{61}$$$.

routes.name: Bit-independent case analysis
routes.description: Recognize that the equation (a|b) - (a&c) = d is independent at each bit position because OR, AND, and the resulting subtraction never interact across bits. Enumerate the four (b_bit, c_bit, d_bit) cases to determine a_bit or detect impossibility.

observations.title: OR and AND are bitwise operations, so consider individual bits
observations.description: Both a|b and a&c are computed independently at each bit position. The value of bit i in (a|b) depends only on bit i of a and bit i of b. Likewise for (a&c). This suggests analyzing the equation one bit at a time.

hints.nudge: What is special about OR and AND that lets you decompose the problem?
hints.question: If you look at just bit position i of the expression (a|b) - (a&c), which bits of a, b, and c does it depend on?
hints.formal: Bit i of (a|b) equals a_i OR b_i, and bit i of (a&c) equals a_i AND c_i. These depend only on a_i, b_i, and c_i respectively, not on any other bit positions.

observations.title: The subtraction never produces a borrow across bits
observations.description: For the bit-by-bit approach to work, the subtraction (a|b) - (a&c) must also be independent per bit. This holds because at every bit position, (a_i OR b_i) is always greater than or equal to (a_i AND c_i). The OR result is 0 or 1, and the AND result is 0 or 1, but the combination never yields 0 - 1 = -1 at any bit. This means no borrow is ever generated, so the subtraction decomposes bitwise.

hints.nudge: For the bitwise decomposition to work for subtraction too, what must be true about the per-bit difference?
hints.question: Can (a_i OR b_i) ever be 0 while (a_i AND c_i) is 1? What would that require of a_i?
hints.formal: If a_i OR b_i = 0, then a_i = 0. But then a_i AND c_i = 0 AND c_i = 0, not 1. So the case (a|b)_i = 0 and (a&c)_i = 1 is impossible. The per-bit difference is always in {0, 1}, never -1, so no borrow propagates.

observations.title: Build a truth table over (a_i, b_i, c_i) to find d_i
observations.description: Since each bit is independent, enumerate all 8 combinations of (a_i, b_i, c_i) in {0,1}^3 and compute d_i = (a_i OR b_i) - (a_i AND c_i). This gives a complete mapping from inputs to output for one bit. The table reveals which value of d_i each (a_i, b_i, c_i) produces.

hints.nudge: There are only 8 possible combinations of three bits. Can you just list them all?
hints.question: For each of the 8 combinations of (a_i, b_i, c_i), what is (a_i|b_i) - (a_i&c_i)? Write out the table.

observations.title: Invert the table: given (b_i, c_i, d_i), determine a_i or detect impossibility
observations.description: The problem gives b, c, d and asks for a. Reorganize the truth table by the known quantities (b_i, c_i, d_i) and read off which value of a_i (if any) produces that combination. For most (b_i, c_i, d_i) triples there is exactly one valid a_i, but two triples are impossible.

hints.nudge: You have the truth table mapping (a_i, b_i, c_i) to d_i. Now flip it: given (b_i, c_i, d_i), what is a_i?
hints.question: Group the 8 rows of your truth table by (b_i, c_i). For each group, which d_i values are achievable with a_i=0 and a_i=1? Are there any (b_i, c_i, d_i) triples with no valid a_i?
hints.formal: b=0,c=0: a=0->d=0, a=1->d=1. b=0,c=1: a=0->d=0, a=1->d=0. b=1,c=0: a=0->d=1, a=1->d=1. b=1,c=1: a=0->d=1, a=1->d=0. So: (b,c,d)=(0,1,1) is impossible (both a values give d=0) and (b,c,d)=(1,0,0) is impossible (both a values give d=1). All other (b,c,d) triples have a unique a.

observations.title: Iterate over all 61 bit positions and assemble a
observations.description: For each bit position from 0 to 60, extract b_i, c_i, d_i. Look up a_i from the case table. If any bit position hits an impossible case, output -1. Otherwise, set the corresponding bit in a and output the result. The total work per test case is O(61), easily handling 10^5 test cases.

hints.nudge: You have a rule for each bit. How do you combine them into the final answer?
hints.question: How do you extract bit i from b, c, d? How do you set bit i in a? What is the loop structure?
hints.formal: Initialize a = 0. For i = 0 to 60: extract b_i = (b >> i) & 1, c_i = (c >> i) & 1, d_i = (d >> i) & 1. Use the lookup to find a_i or detect impossibility. If valid, set a |= (a_i << i). After all bits, output a.
